title: Motorcycle Accidents
description: Motorcycle accidents often result in serious injuries because riders lack the physical protection of an enclosed vehicle. Unique legal challenges — including bias against motorcyclists — make documentation and prompt action especially important.
metaTitle: Motorcycle Accident Guide — Steps, Rights & What to Know
metaDescription: What to do after a motorcycle accident in California or Arizona. Evidence checklist, immediate steps, and guidance on speaking with an attorney.

commonCauses.title: Left-Turn Collisions
commonCauses.description: The most common type of motorcycle accident — a car turning left in front of an oncoming motorcycle at an intersection or driveway.

commonCauses.title: Lane Change Accidents
commonCauses.description: Motorcycles are easily missed in blind spots. Drivers changing lanes without checking are a leading cause of serious motorcycle collisions.

commonCauses.title: Rear-End Collisions
commonCauses.description: Motorcycles stop faster than cars but are more vulnerable when struck from behind. Following-too-close drivers pose a significant risk.

commonCauses.title: Distracted Driving
commonCauses.description: Drivers using phones or otherwise distracted are less likely to notice motorcycles, particularly at intersections and on highways.

commonCauses.title: Road Hazards
commonCauses.description: Gravel, potholes, oil slicks, and uneven pavement that are minor nuisances to cars can cause a motorcyclist to lose control.

commonCauses.title: Speeding
commonCauses.description: Both driver and rider speeding reduce reaction time. High-speed motorcycle crashes carry a high risk of catastrophic injury.

commonCauses.title: DUI / Impairment
commonCauses.description: Impaired drivers have reduced ability to detect motorcycles and respond in time, making them disproportionately involved in fatal motorcycle crashes.

immediateSteps.title: Call 911
immediateSteps.description: Motorcycle accidents frequently result in serious injury. Call 911 even if you feel okay — many serious injuries have delayed symptoms.

immediateSteps.title: Stay at the scene and stay calm
immediateSteps.description: Do not remove your helmet unless there is an immediate airway obstruction and a trained person is present. Stay still if you feel neck or back pain.

immediateSteps.title: Document everything before the scene is cleared
immediateSteps.description: Photograph vehicle positions, damage, road conditions, skid marks, and any defects in the road that may have contributed to the accident.

immediateSteps.title: Get the other driver's information
immediateSteps.description: Name, license number, insurance company, policy number, and contact details. Do not discuss fault at the scene.

immediateSteps.title: Identify witnesses
immediateSteps.description: Get names and contact information from anyone who saw the accident. Bias against motorcyclists makes independent witnesses especially important.

immediateSteps.title: Preserve your helmet and gear
immediateSteps.description: Your helmet, jacket, gloves, and footwear are physical evidence of the accident's severity. Do not discard or repair them.

immediateSteps.title: Seek medical evaluation immediately
immediateSteps.description: Even minor-seeming impacts can cause internal bleeding or traumatic brain injury. Get evaluated the same day.

immediateSteps.title: Notify your insurer
immediateSteps.description: Report the accident to your insurance company. Avoid admitting fault or speculating about injuries.

items: Photos of all vehicles from every angle (before moving)
Photos of road conditions, skid marks, and debris
Photos of your motorcycle damage
Other driver's license, insurance, and contact info
Police report number
Names and contact info of witnesses
Your helmet (preserved — do not clean or repair)
Your riding jacket, gloves, and boots
Photos of road rash, bruising, and visible injuries at the scene
Motorcycle repair estimate or total loss documentation
Emergency room or trauma center records
All follow-up physician and specialist notes
Imaging reports (X-ray, MRI, CT scan)
Physical therapy records
Prescription receipts
Ongoing symptom and recovery journal
All medical bills
Motorcycle repair or replacement documentation
Lost wages documentation from employer
Rental or transportation costs during recovery
Any accident-related out-of-pocket expenses
Dashcam or traffic camera footage
Surveillance video from nearby businesses
Weather and road condition reports for that day
Any prior complaints about the road hazard (if applicable)

timelineRisks.risk: Helmet and gear may be discarded
timelineRisks.action: Preserve all riding gear — it is physical evidence of the crash's severity. Store it in a safe place and do not repair or clean it.
Seek a full medical evaluation, including neurological screening. Traumatic brain injury and internal injuries often present later.
Report to your insurer promptly. Avoid recorded statements to the opposing driver's insurer without guidance.
Secure written witness statements. Download any available dashcam or surveillance footage.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for personal injury
timelineRisks.action: California generally provides 2 years from the accident date to file a personal injury claim. Missing this deadline typically bars the claim.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for personal injury
timelineRisks.action: Arizona also has a 2-year personal injury statute of limitations. Contact an attorney well before this deadline.

insuranceIssues.explanation: Insurance adjusters may attempt to attribute fault to the motorcyclist by suggesting speeding, lane-splitting, or recklessness, even without evidence. Strong documentation counters this.

In California and Arizona, helmet use can affect comparative fault arguments. California requires helmets; Arizona does not for riders over 18, but lack of a helmet may be raised in damages arguments.
Motorcyclists are frequently hit by underinsured or uninsured drivers. Your own UM/UIM coverage may be critical to recovering full compensation.
Motorcycle injuries often involve lengthy recovery. Accepting a quick settlement before understanding the full extent of injuries can leave you without recourse for future medical needs.
Many motorcycle insurance policies have different MedPay or medical payment terms than auto policies. Review your coverage carefully.

whenToGetLawyer: You sustained injuries requiring medical treatment
The other driver's insurer is disputing fault or blaming you
You received a settlement offer before your treatment is complete
The accident involved a road defect or government-maintained roadway
Your injuries have caused extended lost wages or disability
The other driver was uninsured or underinsured
You are unsure how to counter anti-motorcyclist bias in the claims process
